run.initial_example:
Concept: Compound Interest

Example:
Imagine you deposit $100 into a savings account that earns 5% interest per year. With compound interest, you earn interest not only on your original deposit but also on the interest you've already earned.

Year 1:
- Starting amount: $100
- Interest earned (5%): $5
- Ending amount: $105

Year 2:
- Starting amount: $105 (includes last year's interest)
- Interest earned (5%): $5.25
- Ending amount: $110.25

Year 3:
- Starting amount: $110.25
- Interest earned (5%): $5.51
- Ending amount: $115.76

The key difference from simple interest (which would only pay $5 each year, totaling $115 after 3 years) is that the interest itself starts earning interest. This causes your money to grow faster over time.

Key Insight:
Compound interest is earning "interest on interest," which makes your savings grow at an accelerating rate the longer you leave it untouched.
